name: Great Poems
description: The most unforgettable lines of verse ever written, from Frost's diverging roads to Dickinson's feathered hope.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content:
Two roads diverged in a wood, and I—
I took the one less traveled by,
And that has made all the difference.
quotes.notes: The closing lines of Frost's much-quoted (and often misread) meditation on choice and self-mythology.

quotes.content:
And miles to go before I sleep,
And miles to go before I sleep.
quotes.source: Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening (1923)
quotes.notes: The repeated final lines that turn a quiet snowy pause into a reflection on duty and mortality.

quotes.content:
Some say the world will end in fire,
Some say in ice.
quotes.notes: The opening of Frost's nine-line poem weighing desire against hatred as agents of the world's end.

quotes.content:
"Hope" is the thing with feathers –
That perches in the soul –
quotes.notes: Dickinson's famous image of hope as a small bird that sings on without ever asking anything in return.

quotes.content:
Because I could not stop for Death –
He kindly stopped for me –
quotes.source: Because I could not stop for Death (c. 1863)
quotes.notes: The opening of Dickinson's serene carriage ride with a courteous, personified Death.

quotes.content: O Captain! my Captain! our fearful trip is done;
quotes.notes: Whitman's elegy for the assassinated Abraham Lincoln, cast as a ship returning home from a storm.

quotes.content:
Do I contradict myself?
Very well then I contradict myself,
(I am large, I contain multitudes.)
quotes.notes: From the sprawling centerpiece of Leaves of Grass, Whitman's embrace of his own vast contradictions.

quotes.content:
Beauty is truth, truth beauty,—that is all
Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know.
quotes.notes: The urn's famous closing pronouncement, among the most debated lines in English Romantic poetry.

quotes.content: A thing of beauty is a joy for ever:
quotes.notes: The celebrated opening line of Keats's long mythological poem about the moon goddess and a shepherd.

quotes.content:
I wandered lonely as a cloud
That floats on high o'er vales and hills,
quotes.source: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud (1807)
quotes.notes: The opening of Wordsworth's 'Daffodils,' inspired by a host of golden flowers in England's Lake District.

quotes.content:
Theirs not to make reply,
Theirs not to reason why,
Theirs but to do and die:
quotes.source: The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854)
quotes.notes: Tennyson's tribute to the doomed cavalry charge during the Battle of Balaclava in the Crimean War.

quotes.content: To strive, to seek, to find, and not to yield.
quotes.notes: The resolute final line of Tennyson's dramatic monologue spoken by an aged, restless Ulysses.

quotes.content:
'Tis better to have loved and lost
Than never to have loved at all.
quotes.notes: From Tennyson's long elegy mourning his friend Arthur Henry Hallam.

quotes.content:
If you can keep your head when all about you
Are losing theirs and blaming it on you;
quotes.notes: The opening of Kipling's catalogue of stoic virtues, addressed as advice to his son.

quotes.content:
Do not go gentle into that good night,
Rage, rage against the dying of the light.
quotes.source: Do not go gentle into that good night (1951)
quotes.notes: The opening and closing refrains of Thomas's villanelle urging his dying father to fight death.

quotes.content:
You may write me down in history
With your bitter, twisted lies,
You may trod me in the very dirt
But still, like dust, I'll rise.
quotes.notes: The defiant opening stanza of Angelou's anthem of resilience against oppression.

quotes.content:
Tyger Tyger, burning bright,
In the forests of the night;
quotes.notes: From Blake's Songs of Experience, questioning what creator could forge such fearful symmetry.

quotes.content:
To see a World in a Grain of Sand
And a Heaven in a Wild Flower,
quotes.notes: The opening couplet of Blake's poem on finding the infinite within the smallest things.

quotes.content:
Hold fast to dreams
For if dreams die
Life is a broken-winged bird
That cannot fly.
quotes.notes: Hughes's short, much-loved exhortation to cling to one's dreams.

quotes.content:
What happens to a dream deferred?
Does it dry up
like a raisin in the sun?
quotes.notes: The opening of Hughes's poem on postponed hopes, whose image gave Lorraine Hansberry her play's title.

quotes.content:
Things fall apart; the centre cannot hold;
Mere anarchy is loosed upon the world,
quotes.notes: Yeats's vision of a civilization unraveling, written in the aftermath of the First World War.

quotes.content:
But one man loved the pilgrim soul in you,
And loved the sorrows of your changing face;
quotes.notes: Yeats's tender address imagining a beloved grown old, recalling the one who loved her truly.

quotes.content:
Remember me when I am gone away,
Gone far away into the silent land;
quotes.notes: The opening of Rossetti's sonnet that gently releases the living from grief over the dead.

quotes.content:
My name is Ozymandias, king of kings:
Look on my works, ye Mighty, and despair!
quotes.notes: The boast inscribed on a ruined statue in Shelley's sonnet on the vanity of empire and power.

quotes.content:
She walks in beauty, like the night
Of cloudless climes and starry skies;
quotes.notes: Byron's lyric praising a woman in mourning dress whose beauty he glimpsed at a party.

quotes.content:
I am the master of my fate,
I am the captain of my soul.
quotes.notes: The closing lines of Henley's defiant poem, written as he recovered from a tubercular amputation.

quotes.content:
O my Luve is like a red, red rose
That's newly sprung in June;
quotes.notes: The opening of Burns's Scots love song, drawn from traditional folk sources.

quotes.content: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
quotes.notes: The famous opening of Barrett Browning's sonnet to her husband, the poet Robert Browning.

quotes.content: The world is charged with the grandeur of God.
quotes.notes: The arresting first line of Hopkins's sonnet on the divine that flames out through nature.

quotes.content:
Death, be not proud, though some have called thee
Mighty and dreadful, for thou art not so;
quotes.source: Holy Sonnets: Death, be not proud (c. 1610)
quotes.notes: Donne's bold address that taunts death as a powerless slave to fate and chance.

quotes.content:
This is the way the world ends
Not with a bang but a whimper.
quotes.notes: The famous closing lines of Eliot's bleak poem about spiritual emptiness.

quotes.content:
The fog comes
on little cat feet.
quotes.notes: Sandburg's tiny imagist poem comparing a rolling fog to a silent, watchful cat.

quotes.content:
My candle burns at both ends;
It will not last the night;
quotes.notes: The opening of Millay's brief, brilliant ode to living fast and burning bright.

quotes.content:
I'm Nobody! Who are you?
Are you – Nobody – too?
quotes.notes: The poem's famous opening couplet; only these two lines are quoted since a later line has a disputed manuscript-vs-published wording.

quotes.content:
The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est
Pro patria mori.
quotes.source: Dulce et Decorum Est (written 1917, published in Poems, 1920)
quotes.notes: The closing indictment of Horace's maxim ('it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country'), drafted at Craiglockhart War Hospital and published posthumously.
